title: Bishop Blougramï؟½s Apology
content:
آ آ آ آ No more wine? then weï؟½ll push back chairs and talk.
آ آ آ آ A final glass for me, thoï؟½; cool, iï؟½faith!
آ آ آ آ We ought to have our Abbey back, you see.
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s different, preaching in basilicas,
آ آ آ آ And doing duty in some masterpiece
آ آ آ آ Like this of brother Puginï؟½s, bless his heart!
آ آ آ آ I doubt if theyï؟½re half baked, those chalk rosettes,
آ آ آ آ Ciphers and stucco-twiddlings everywhere;
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s just like breathing in a lime-kiln: eh?
آ آ آ آ These hot long ceremonies of our church
آ آ آ آ Cost us a little oh, they pay the price,
آ آ آ آ You take me amply pay it! Now, weï؟½ll talk.

آ آ آ آ So, you despise me, Mr. Gigadibs.
آ آ آ آ No deprecation, nay, I beg you, sir!
آ آ آ آ Beside ï؟½tis our engagement: donï؟½t you know,
آ آ آ آ I promised, if youï؟½d watch a dinner out,
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½d see truth dawn together? truth that peeps
آ آ آ آ Over the glassesï؟½ edge when dinnerï؟½s done,
آ آ آ آ And body gets its sop and holds its noise
آ آ آ آ And leaves soul free a little. Nowï؟½s the time
آ آ آ آ ï؟½T is break of day! You do despise me then.
آ آ آ آ And if I say, ï؟½despise me,ï؟½ never fear
آ آ آ آ I know you do not in a certain sense
آ آ آ آ Not in my arm-chair, for example: here,
آ آ آ آ I well imagine you respect my place
آ آ آ آ ( Status, entourage, worldly circumstance)
آ آ آ آ Quite to its value very much indeed:
آ آ آ آ  Are up to the protesting eyes of you
آ آ آ آ In pride at being seated here for once
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ll turn it to such capital account!
آ آ آ آ When somebody, through years and years to come,
آ آ آ آ Hints of the bishop, names me thatï؟½s enough:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Blougram? I knew himï؟½ (into it you slide)
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Dined with him once, a Corpus Christi Day,
آ آ آ آ All alone, we two; heï؟½s a clever man:
آ آ آ آ And after dinner, why, the wine you know,
آ آ آ آ Oh, there was wine, and good! what with the wine . .
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Faith, we began upon all sorts of talk!
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s no bad fellow, Blougram; he had seen
آ آ آ آ Something of mine he relished, some review:
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s quite above their humbug in his heart,
آ آ آ آ Half-said as much, indeed the thingï؟½s his trade.
آ آ آ آ I warrant, Blougramï؟½s sceptical at times:
آ آ آ آ How otherwise? I liked him, I confess!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Che chï؟½ï؟½, my dear sir, as we say at Rome,
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t you protest now! Itï؟½s fair give and take;
آ آ آ آ You have had your turn and spoken your home-truths
آ آ آ آ The handï؟½s mine now, and here you follow suit.

آ آ آ آ Thus much conceded, still the first fact stays
آ آ آ آ You do despise me; your ideal of life
آ آ آ آ Is not the bishopï؟½s: you would not be I
آ آ آ آ You would like better to be Goethe, now,
آ آ آ آ Or Buonaparte or, bless me, lower still,
آ آ آ آ Count Dï؟½Orsay, so you did what you preferred,
آ آ آ آ Spoke as you thought, and, as you cannot help,
آ آ آ آ Believed or disbelieved, no matter what,
آ آ آ آ So long as on that point, whateï؟½er it was,
آ آ آ آ You loosed your mind, were whole and sole yourself.
آ آ آ آ  That, my ideal never can include,
آ آ آ آ Upon that element of truth and worth
آ آ آ آ Never be based! for say they make me Pope
آ آ آ آ (They canï؟½t suppose it for our argument!)
آ آ آ آ Why, there Iï؟½m at my tetherï؟½s end, Iï؟½ve reached
آ آ آ آ My height, and not a height which pleases you:
آ آ آ آ An unbelieving Pope wonï؟½t do, you say.
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s like those eerie stories nurses tell,
آ آ آ آ Of how some actor on a stage played Death,
آ آ آ آ With pasteboard crown, sham orb and tinselled dart,
آ آ آ آ And called himself the monarch of the world;
آ آ آ آ Then, going in the tire-room afterward,
آ آ آ آ Because the play was done, to shift himself,
آ آ آ آ Got touched upon the sleeve familiarly,
آ آ آ آ The moment he had shut the closet door,
آ آ آ آ By Death himself. Thus God might touch a Pope
آ آ آ آ At unawares, ask what his baubles mean,
آ آ آ آ And whose part he presumed to play just now?
آ آ آ آ Best be yourself, imperial, plain and true!

آ آ آ آ So, drawing comfortable breath again,
آ آ آ آ You weigh and find, whatever more or less
آ آ آ آ I boast of my ideal realized,
آ آ آ آ Is nothing in the balance when opposed
آ آ آ آ To your ideal, your grand simple life,
آ آ آ آ Of which you will not realize one jot.
آ آ آ آ I am much, you are nothing; you would be all,
آ آ آ آ I would be merely much you beat me there.

آ آ آ آ No, friend, you do not beat me: hearken why!
آ آ آ آ The common problem, yours, mine, every oneï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ Is not to fancy what were fair in life
آ آ آ آ Provided it could be, but, finding first
آ آ آ آ What may be, then find how to make it fair
آ آ آ آ Up to our means: a very different thing!
آ آ آ آ No abstract intellectual plan of life
آ آ آ آ Quite irrespective of lifeï؟½s plainest laws,
آ آ آ آ But one, a man, who is man and nothing more,
آ آ آ آ May lead within a world which (by your leave)
آ آ آ آ Is Rome or London, not Foolï؟½s-paradise.
آ آ آ آ Embellish Rome, idealize away,
آ آ آ آ Make paradise of London if you can,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re welcome, nay, youï؟½re wise.

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ A simile!
آ آ آ آ We mortals cross the ocean of this world
آ آ آ آ Each in his average cabin of a life
آ آ آ آ The bestï؟½s not big, the worst yields elbow-room.
آ آ آ آ Now for our six monthsï؟½ voyage how prepare?
آ آ آ آ You come on shipboard with a landsmanï؟½s list
آ آ آ آ Of things he calls convenient so they are!
آ آ آ آ An India screen is pretty furniture,
آ آ آ آ A piano-forte is a fine resource,
آ آ آ آ All Balzacï؟½s novels occupy one shelf,
آ آ آ آ The new edition fifty volumes long;
آ آ آ آ And little Greek books, with the funny type
آ آ آ آ They get up well at Leipsic, fill the next
آ آ آ آ Go on! slabbed marble, what a bath it makes!
آ آ آ آ And Parmaï؟½s pride, the Jerome, let us add!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twere pleasant could Correggioï؟½s fleeting glow
آ آ آ آ Hang full in face of one whereï؟½er one roams,
آ آ آ آ Since he more than the others brings with him
آ آ آ آ Italyï؟½s self, the marvellous Modenese!
آ آ آ آ Yet ï؟½twas not on your list before, perhaps.
آ آ آ آ  Alas, friend, hereï؟½s the agent . . . isï؟½t the name?
آ آ آ آ The captain, or whoeverï؟½s master here
آ آ آ آ You see him screw his face up; whatï؟½s his cry
آ آ آ آ Ere you set foot on shipboard? ï؟½Six feet square!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ If you wonï؟½t understand what six feet mean,
آ آ آ آ Compute and purchase stores accordingly
آ آ آ آ And if, in pique because he overhauls
آ آ آ آ Your Jerome, piano, bath, you come on board
آ آ آ آ Bare why, you cut a figure at the first
آ آ آ آ While sympathetic landsmen see you off;
آ آ آ آ Not afterward, when long ere half seas over,
آ آ آ آ You peep up from your utterly naked boards
آ آ آ آ Into some snug and well-appointed berth,
آ آ آ آ Like mine for instance (try the cooler jug
آ آ آ آ Put back the other, but donï؟½t jog the ice!)
آ آ آ آ And mortified you mutter ï؟½Well and good;
آ آ آ آ He sits enjoying his sea-furniture;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis stout and proper, and thereï؟½s store of it:
آ آ آ آ Though Iï؟½ve the better notion, all agree,
آ آ آ آ Of fitting rooms up. Hang the carpenter,
آ آ آ آ Neat ship-shape fixings and contrivances
آ آ آ آ I would have brought my Jerome, frame and all!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And meantime you bring nothing: never mind
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ve proved your artist-nature: what you donï؟½t
آ آ آ آ You might bring, so despise me, as I say.

آ آ آ آ Now come, letï؟½s backward to the starting-place.
آ آ آ آ See my way: weï؟½re two college friends, suppose
آ آ آ آ Prepare together for our voyage, then;
آ آ آ آ Each note and check the other in his work,
آ آ آ آ Hereï؟½s mine, a bishopï؟½s outfit; criticize!
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s wrong? why wonï؟½t you be a bishop too?

آ آ آ آ Why first, you donï؟½t believe, you donï؟½t and canï؟½t,
آ آ آ آ (Not statedly, that is, and fixedly
آ آ آ آ And absolutely and exclusively)
آ آ آ آ In any revelation called divine.
آ آ آ آ No dogmas nail your faith; and what remains
آ آ آ آ But say so, like the honest man you are?
آ آ آ آ First, therefore, overhaul theology!
آ آ آ آ Nay, I too, not a fool, you please to think,
آ آ آ آ Must find believing every whit as hard:
آ آ آ آ And if I do not frankly say as much,
آ آ آ آ The ugly consequence is clear enough.

آ آ آ آ Now wait, my friend: well, I do not believe
آ آ آ آ If youï؟½ll accept no faith that is not fixed,
آ آ آ آ Absolute and exclusive, as you say.
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re wrong I mean to prove it in due time.
آ آ آ آ Meanwhile, I know where difficulties lie
آ آ آ آ I could not, cannot solve, nor ever shall,
آ آ آ آ So give up hope accordingly to solve
آ آ آ آ (To you, and over the wine). Our dogmas then
آ آ آ آ With both of us, though in unlike degree,
آ آ آ آ Missing full credence overboard with them!
آ آ آ آ I mean to meet you on your own premise
آ آ آ آ Good, there go mine in company with yours!

آ آ آ آ And now what are we? unbelievers both,
آ آ آ آ Calm and complete, determinately fixed
آ آ آ آ To-day, to-morrow and for ever, pray?
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ll guarantee me that? Not so, I think!
آ آ آ آ In no wise! all weï؟½ve gained is, that belief,
آ آ آ آ As unbelief before, shakes us by fits,
آ آ آ آ Confounds us like its predecessor. Whereï؟½s
آ آ آ آ The gain? how can we guard our unbelief,
آ آ آ آ Make it bear fruit to us? the problem here.
آ آ آ آ Just when we are safest, thereï؟½s a sunset-touch,
آ آ آ آ A fancy from a flower-bell, some oneï؟½s death,
آ آ آ آ A chorus-ending from Euripides,
آ آ آ آ And thatï؟½s enough for fifty hopes and fears
آ آ آ آ As old and new at once as natureï؟½s self,
آ آ آ آ To rap and knock and enter in our soul,
آ آ آ آ Take hands and dance there, a fantastic ring,
آ آ آ آ Round the ancient idol, on his base again,
آ آ آ آ The grand Perhaps! We look on helplessly.
آ آ آ آ There the old misgivings, crooked questions are
آ آ آ آ This good God, what he could do, if he would,
آ آ آ آ Would, if he could then must have done long since:
آ آ آ آ If so, when, where and how? some way must be,
آ آ آ آ Once feel about, and soon or late you hit
آ آ آ آ Some sense, in which it might be, after all.
آ آ آ آ Why not, ï؟½The Way, the Truth, the Life?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  That way
آ آ آ آ Over the mountain, which who stands upon
آ آ آ آ Is apt to doubt if it be meant for a road;
آ آ آ آ While, if he views it from the waste itself,
آ آ آ آ Up goes the line there, plain from base to brow,
آ آ آ آ Not vague, mistakeable! whatï؟½s a break or two
آ آ آ آ Seen from the unbroken desert either side?
آ آ آ آ And then (to bring in fresh philosophy)
آ آ آ آ What if the breaks themselves should prove at last
آ آ آ آ The most consummate of contrivances
آ آ آ آ To train a manï؟½s eye, teach him what is faith?
آ آ آ آ And so we stumble at truthï؟½s very test!
آ آ آ آ All we have gained then by our unbelief
آ آ آ آ Is a life of doubt diversified by faith,
آ آ آ آ For one of faith diversified by doubt:
آ آ آ آ We called the chess-board white, we call it black.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Well,ï؟½ you rejoin, ï؟½the endï؟½s no worse, at least;
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ve reason for both colours on the board:
آ آ آ آ Why not confess then, where I drop the faith
آ آ آ آ And you the doubt, that Iï؟½m as right as you?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Because, friend, in the next place, this being so,
آ آ آ آ And both things even, faith and unbelief
آ آ آ آ Left to a manï؟½s choice, weï؟½ll proceed a step,
آ آ آ آ Returning to our image, which I like.

آ آ آ آ A manï؟½s choice, yes but a cabin-passengerï؟½s
آ آ آ آ The man made for the special life oï؟½ the world
آ آ آ آ Do you forget him? I remember though!
آ آ آ آ Consult our shipï؟½s conditions and you find
آ آ آ آ One and but one choice suitable to all;
آ آ آ آ The choice, that you unluckily prefer,
آ آ آ آ Turning things topsy-turvy they or it
آ آ آ آ Going to the ground. Belief or unbelief
آ آ آ آ Bears upon life, determines its whole course,
آ آ آ آ Begins at its beginning. See the world
آ آ آ آ Such as it is, you made it not, nor I;
آ آ آ آ I mean to take it as it is, and you,
آ آ آ آ Not so youï؟½ll take it, though you get nought else.
آ آ آ آ I know the special kind of life I like,
آ آ آ آ What suits the most my idiosyncrasy,
آ آ آ آ Brings out the best of me and bears me fruit
آ آ آ آ In power, peace, pleasantness and length of days.
آ آ آ آ I find that positive belief does this
آ آ آ آ For me, and unbelief, no whit of this.
آ آ آ آ  For you, it does, however? that, weï؟½ll try!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis clear, I cannot lead my life, at least,
آ آ آ آ Induce the world to let me peaceably,
آ آ آ آ Without declaring at the outset, ï؟½Friends,
آ آ آ آ I absolutely and peremptorily
آ آ آ آ Believe!ï؟½ I say, faith is my waking life:
آ آ آ آ One sleeps, indeed, and dreams at intervals,
آ آ آ آ We know, but wakingï؟½s the main point with us
آ آ آ آ And my provisionï؟½s for lifeï؟½s waking part.
آ آ آ آ Accordingly, I use heart, head and hand
آ آ آ آ All day, I build, scheme, study, and make friends;
آ آ آ آ And when night overtakes me, down I lie,
آ آ آ آ Sleep, dream a little, and get done with it,
آ آ آ آ The sooner the better, to begin afresh.
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s midnight doubt before the dayspringï؟½s faith?
آ آ آ آ You, the philosopher, that disbelieve,
آ آ آ آ That recognize the night, give dreams their weight
آ آ آ آ To be consistent you should keep your bed,
آ آ آ آ Abstain from healthy acts that prove you man,
آ آ آ آ For fear you drowse perhaps at unawares!
آ آ آ آ And certainly at night youï؟½ll sleep and dream,
آ آ آ آ Live through the day and bustle as you please.
آ آ آ آ And so you live to sleep as I to wake,
آ آ آ آ To unbelieve as I to still believe?
آ آ آ آ Well, and the common sense oï؟½ the world calls you
آ آ آ آ Bed-ridden, and its good things come to me.
آ آ آ آ Its estimation, which is half the fight,
آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s the first-cabin comfort I secure
آ آ آ آ The next . . . but you perceive with half an eye!
آ آ آ آ Come, come, itï؟½s best believing, if we may;
آ آ آ آ You canï؟½t but own that!

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Next, concede again,
آ آ آ آ If once we choose belief, on all accounts
آ آ آ آ We canï؟½t be too decisive in our faith,
آ آ آ آ Conclusive and exclusive in its terms,
آ آ آ آ To suit the world which gives us the good things.
آ آ آ آ In every manï؟½s career are certain points
آ آ آ آ Whereon he dares not be indifferent;

آ آ آ آ Open great eyes at you and even butt,
آ آ آ آ And thereupon you like your mates so well
آ آ آ آ You cannot please yourself, offending them
آ آ آ آ Though when they seem exorbitantly sheep,
آ آ آ آ You weigh your pleasure with their butts and bleats
آ آ آ آ And strike the balance. Sometimes certain fears
آ آ آ آ Restrain you real checks since you find them so
آ آ آ آ Sometimes you please yourself and nothing checks;
آ آ آ آ And thus you graze through life with not one lie,
آ آ آ آ And like it best.

آ آ آ آ But do you, in truthï؟½s name?
آ آ آ آ If so, you beat which means you are not I
آ آ آ آ Who needs must make earth mine and feed my fill
آ آ آ آ Not simply unbutted at, unbickered with,
آ آ آ آ But motioned to the velvet of the sward
آ آ آ آ By those obsequious wethersï؟½ very selves.
آ آ آ آ Look at me, sir; my age is double yours:
آ آ آ آ At yours, I knew beforehand, so enjoyed,
آ آ آ آ What now I should be as, permit the word,
آ آ آ آ I pretty well imagine your whole range
آ آ آ آ And stretch of tether twenty years to come.
آ آ آ آ We both have minds and bodies much alike:
آ آ آ آ In truthï؟½s name, donï؟½t you want my bishopric,
آ آ آ آ My daily bread, my influence and my state?
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re young. Iï؟½m old; you must be old one day;
آ آ آ آ Will you find then, as I do hour by hour,
آ آ آ آ Women their lovers kneel to, who cut curls
آ آ آ آ From your fat lap-dogï؟½s ear to grace a brooch
آ آ آ آ Dukes, who petition just to kiss your ring
آ آ آ آ With much beside you know or may conceive?
آ آ آ آ Suppose we die to-night: well, here am I,
آ آ آ آ Such were my gains, life bore this fruit to me,
آ آ آ آ While writing all the same my articles
آ آ آ آ On music, poetry, the fictile vase
آ آ آ آ Found at Albano, or Anacreonï؟½s Greek.
آ آ آ آ But you the highest honour in your life,
آ آ آ آ The thing youï؟½ll crown yourself with, all your days,
آ آ آ آ Is dining here and drinking this last glass
آ آ آ آ I pour you out in sign of amity
آ آ آ آ Before we part for ever. Of your power
آ آ آ آ And social influence, worldly worth in short,
آ آ آ آ Judge whatï؟½s my estimation by the fact,

آ آ آ آ Hint secrecy on one of all these words!
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re shrewd and know that should you publish one
آ آ آ آ The world would brand the lie my enemies first,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Whoï؟½d sneer the bishopï؟½s an arch-hypocrite
آ آ آ آ And knave perhaps, but not so frank a fool.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Whereas I should not dare for both my ears
آ آ آ آ Breathe one such syllable, smile one such smile,
آ آ آ آ Before the chaplain who reflects myself
آ آ آ آ My shadeï؟½s so much more potent than your flesh.
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s your reward, self-abnegating friend?
آ آ آ آ Stood you confessed of those exceptional
آ آ آ آ And privileged great natures that dwarf mine

آ آ آ آ A poet just about to print his ode,
آ آ آ آ A statesman with a scheme to stop this war,

آ آ آ آ I should have nothing to object! such men
آ آ آ آ Carry the fire, all things grow warm to them,

آ آ آ آ But you, youï؟½re just as little those as I
آ آ آ آ You, Gigadibs, who, thirty years of age,
آ آ آ آ Write statedly for Blackwoodï؟½s Magazine,

آ آ آ آ Unseized by the Germans yet which view youï؟½ll print

آ آ آ آ That lively lightsome article we took

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The Slum and Cellar, or Whitechapel life

آ آ آ آ  Success I recognize and compliment,
آ آ آ آ And therefore give you, if you choose, three words
آ آ آ آ (The card and pencil-scratch is quite enough)
آ آ آ آ Which whether here, in Dublin or New York,

آ آ آ آ In all our own reviews and some not ours.
آ آ آ آ Go write your lively sketches! be the first
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Blougram, or The Eccentric Confidenceï؟½
آ آ آ آ Or better simply say, ï؟½The Outward-bound.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Why, men as soon would throw it in my teeth

آ آ آ آ About me on the church-door opposite.
آ آ آ آ You will not wait for that experience though,
آ آ آ آ I fancy, howsoever you decide,

آ آ آ آ Over his wine so smiled and talked his hour
آ آ آ آ Sylvester Blougram, styled in partibus
آ آ آ آ Episcopus, nec non (the deuce knows what
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s changed to by our novel hierarchy)

آ آ آ آ Long crumpled, till creased consciousness lay smooth.

آ آ آ آ For Blougram, he believed, say, half he spoke.
آ آ آ آ The other portion, as he shaped it thus
آ آ آ آ For argumentatory purposes,
آ آ آ آ He felt his foe was foolish to dispute.
آ آ آ آ Some arbitrary accidental thoughts
آ آ آ آ That crossed his mind, amusing because new,
آ آ آ آ He chose to represent as fixtures there,
آ آ آ آ Invariable convictions (such they seemed
آ آ آ آ Beside his interlocutorï؟½s loose cards
آ آ آ آ Flung daily down, and not the same way twice)
آ آ آ آ While certain hell deep instincts, manï؟½s weak tongue
آ آ آ آ Is never bold to utter in their truth

آ آ آ آ ï؟½On the whole,ï؟½ he thought, ï؟½I justify myself
آ آ آ آ On every point where cavillers like this
آ آ آ آ Oppugn my life: he tries one kind of fence,
آ آ آ آ I close, heï؟½s worsted, thatï؟½s enough for him.
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s on the ground: if ground should break away

آ آ آ آ His ground was over mine and broke the first:
آ آ آ آ So, let him sit with me this many a year!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He did not sit five minutes. Just a week
آ آ آ آ Sufficed his sudden healthy vehemence.
آ آ آ آ Something had struck him in the ï؟½Outward-boundï؟½

meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
